items.title: CPI posts first negative monthly print since May 2020
items.idea: Headline CPI fell 0.4% m/m vs. -0.1% expected — a 3x downside miss. This is the first negative print since May 2020 and the largest decline since April 2020. Of 91 negative m/m CPI prints in the last 80 years, 46 occurred within a year of a recession; Bespoke isolates the 10 sharp (≥0.3%) declines that happened outside recession windows to study forward S&P 500 returns.
items.angle: The market wants to read this as pure good news for rate cuts. The historical base rate says non-recession negative-CPI prints have mixed near-term but strong medium-term equity outcomes — worth checking Bespoke's actual return table before assuming this is unambiguously bullish.
items.tweetA: CPI just posted its first negative monthly print since May 2020. Of the 10 times this happened outside a recession in 80 years, the base rate leans toward strong forward S&P returns. Fade the 'this confirms a slowdown' narrative — the data says otherwise. $SPY
items.tweetB: -0.4% CPI m/m against a -0.1% consensus is a 3x miss to the downside on inflation. The Fed still isn't cutting in July if Warsh gets his way. The market is about to learn the difference between 'soft landing' and 'no landing, just falling prices.'

charts.desc: Table of 6 historical non-recession CPI decline periods with S&P 500 3/6/12-month forward returns (behind paywall in this preview).

items.title: CPI softness has real breadth — not just one noisy component
items.idea: Only 43% of the CPI basket is running above 2.5% annualized inflation — the lowest share since Feb 2025. 47% of the basket posted outright negative m/m prices, the most since Aug 2024. Fed Governor Waller reportedly emphasized this breadth angle specifically.
items.angle: The -0.4% headline print gets all the attention, but the breadth data is the more durable disinflation signal — it's much harder to dismiss as a one-off than a single soft component.
items.tweetA: 43% of the CPI basket is running above 2.5% annualized — the lowest share since Feb '25. 47% of components actually fell month over month, the most since Aug 2024. This isn't a headline fluke, it's broad-based disinflation.
items.tweetB: Everyone's staring at the -0.4% CPI headline. The real story is underneath: nearly half the basket posted outright price declines. If Waller wanted ammunition to argue for a cut, he just got the best breadth chart of the year.

charts.desc: 🔥 Chart showing share of CPI basket above 2.5% annualized and share with negative m/m prints — both at multi-month/year extremes.

items.title: IBM's worst single day since 1987 — worse than Black Monday
items.idea: IBM pre-announced 8 days ahead of its scheduled report: revenue missed by 4%, down 7% YoY. The stock opened down 22% and closed down 25% — surpassing the 22.96% Black Monday crash of October 1987 as IBM's single worst trading day ever. In nearly 60 years of trading, IBM has fallen double digits in a day only 8 times, including today.
items.angle: This is a pure market-structure data point — the magnitude alone (beating a 1987 crash-day record) makes it a standalone story independent of the AI-capex-rotation narrative other sources are using to explain it.
items.tweetA: IBM just had a worse day than Black Monday 1987. -25% today vs. -22.96% in Oct '87 — IBM's worst single session in nearly 60 years of trading, and only the 8th time it's ever dropped double digits in a day. $IBM
items.tweetB: A quarter-trillion-dollar company shed roughly $50B in market cap in one session off an 8-day-early earnings warning. When the pre-announcement itself is the crash catalyst, that's not a valuation reset — that's a credibility reset.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: historical table of IBM's prior 10%+ single-day drops and subsequent performance.

items.title: Nadella's 'Reverse Information Paradox' essay is Palantir's pitch with a Microsoft logo
items.idea: Nadella published an essay arguing enterprises risk losing institutional knowledge if they trust frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic) with their data — essentially the same 'Us vs Them' argument Palantir's Alex Karp has been making. Parekh's tell: Microsoft still relies on OpenAI's models for the next five years even while telling enterprises not to trust frontier labs with theirs.
items.angle: Microsoft is trying to have it both ways — reselling frontier-lab capability while marketing against the frontier-lab trust model. Watch whether enterprise buyers actually shift budget toward Microsoft-hosted/proprietary stacks versus OpenAI/Anthropic direct.
items.tweetA: Nadella's new essay is basically Palantir's pitch with a Microsoft logo: don't hand your data to frontier labs, they'll cannibalize you with it. Karp's been running this argument for two years. Now the platform companies are turning it into a moat. $MSFT $PLTR
items.tweetB: The tell in Nadella's essay: Microsoft still needs OpenAI's models for the next five years even while telling enterprises not to trust frontier labs with their data. That's not conviction, that's hedging one bet while marketing the other side of it.

items.title: Meta goes community-first in Louisiana right as NY freezes AI data centers
items.idea: Meta is putting roughly $40B of its ~$145B annual data-center buildout into Louisiana using a deliberately community-first, local-approval approach — a shift from its usual pedal-to-the-metal posture. This comes the same week New York's Gov. Hochul enacted the first state-level one-year moratorium on new large AI data centers.
items.angle: Permitting and local political risk are becoming a real, priced variable in the AI capex model — not just a chips/power story. Site-selection strategy is now a genuine competitive differentiator among hyperscalers.
items.tweetA: New York just became the first state to freeze new AI data centers for a year. Meta's response: quietly go community-first in Louisiana with $40B in spend instead of steamrolling local opposition. Permitting risk just became a real line item in the AI capex model.
items.tweetB: The AI buildout bottleneck in a lot of places isn't chips anymore — it's zoning boards. One state moratorium and every hyperscaler's site-selection team just got a new veto player to manage before groundbreaking.

items.title: Even Nvidia is internally compute-rationed — 'RAMageddon' hits the chipmaker too
items.idea: In an interview about Nvidia's AI-Defined-Vehicles unit, Parekh notes that Nvidia's own internal AI teams have to compete for compute allocation, with Jensen Huang personally involved in allocation decisions. Nvidia's ADV platform is now deployed by over 80% of global automakers, including China's BYD.
items.angle: If the company manufacturing the chips is itself supply-constrained on using them internally, that's a stronger signal on the depth of the compute shortage than any hyperscaler capex guide.
items.tweetA: Even Nvidia's own AI teams have to fight Jensen for compute allocation internally. If the company that MAKES the chips is supply-constrained on its own hardware, what does that say about everyone else's roadmap for the next 2-3 years? $NVDA
items.tweetB: 'RAMageddon' isn't just a memory story for Samsung and Micron. Reporting suggests Nvidia is internally compute-constrained too — the scarcity isn't isolated to one layer of the AI stack, it's the whole stack, top to bottom.

items.title: New Jersey nears a Lidar mandate that could hit Tesla's camera-only bet
items.idea: New Jersey is close to requiring Lidar for self-driving cars and robotaxis, with New York considering similar legislation. Tesla's entire autonomy stack is camera-only, betting against sensor redundancy — while Nvidia's Lidar-plus-camera ADV platform already runs in 80%+ of global automakers including BYD.
items.angle: If one state domino falls on a Lidar mandate, Tesla faces a binary choice: retrofit its hardware roadmap or forfeit state-level robotaxi/Level 4-5 approval in major markets.
items.tweetA: New Jersey is close to mandating Lidar for robotaxis. New York may follow. Tesla's entire self-driving bet is camera-only — if one state domino falls, Tesla either adds Lidar or forfeits Level 4/5 approval in major state markets. $TSLA
items.tweetB: Nvidia's ADV platform already runs Lidar-plus-camera stacks in 80%+ of global automakers, including BYD. Tesla is the outlier betting against sensor redundancy — and state legislators are about to turn that bet into a legal liability, not just an engineering choice.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom™
items.title: Goldman's 49% EPS beat masks a market getting narrower, not broader
items.idea: Goldman Sachs posted $20.98 EPS vs. $14.10 consensus (a ~49% beat) on $20.3B revenue, with IB fees of $3.4B and buybacks exceeding $4B. Yet the same session, hedge funds bought tech and industrials while long-only investors sold financials, healthcare, and consumer discretionary. The S&P 493 (ex-mega-cap) was roughly flat — all index gains came from the AI complex.
items.angle: A blowout bank quarter didn't broaden participation — it happened alongside a rotation that made breadth worse, not better. Watch whether 'strong earnings' headlines keep masking a market that's structurally thinner underneath.
items.tweetA: Goldman printed $20.98 EPS against $14.10 consensus — a 49% beat. Same day, hedge funds bought tech and industrials while long-only sold financials, healthcare, and discretionary. The 'strong earnings' tape and the positioning tape are telling two different stories. $GS
items.tweetB: The S&P 493 (ex-mega-cap) was roughly flat today. Every point of index gain came from the AI complex. Goldman's blowout quarter didn't broaden the market — it just added another beam to a roof already resting on six stocks.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: Goldman earnings breakdown and breadth/flow tables.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom™
items.title: Trump's Hormuz cargo fee dies fast, but distillates say the real risk didn't
items.idea: Trump's proposed 20% cargo fee on tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz was abandoned within days after Gulf-ally pushback — ClearView Energy estimated it would've cost ~$30M per supertanker and added ~37 cents to a gallon of gas. Meanwhile heating oil futures hit fresh conflict-era highs even as Brent settled a comparatively modest ~2% higher at $84.73.
items.angle: The TACO pattern (threaten, spook markets, cave, rebrand as a deal) played out again on the fee — but distillates, which nobody trades on headlines, are the cleaner read on actual physical tightness building beneath the crude price.
items.tweetA: Trump floated a 20% cargo fee on tankers transiting Hormuz — ClearView Energy pegged that at ~37 cents added to a gallon of gas. He killed it within days after Gulf allies pushed back. Classic TACO: threaten, spook the tape, cave, rebrand as a trade deal. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: Crude is calm-ish at $84.73 Brent. Heating oil just hit fresh conflict-era highs. Nobody trades distillates for headlines, which is exactly why that move is the signal worth watching and the crude move is the noise.

items.title: Markets still price 80% odds of a Fed hike by year-end despite the coldest CPI since 2020
items.idea: Even after the softest CPI print since April 2020, Sethi notes markets are still pricing roughly 80% odds of a Fed hike by year-end. Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's testimony reiterated a hawkish inflation-fighting stance despite the data. WTI briefly crossed $80 the same day on renewed Hormuz/Iran risk.
items.angle: Soft CPI bought the Fed a pass on a July move, not a pivot away from hikes altogether — the year-end hike odds barely moved, which is the more important number than the headline CPI beat.
items.tweetA: Markets are still pricing 80% odds of a Fed hike by year-end — even after the coldest CPI print since April 2020. The soft data bought Warsh a pass on July, not a pivot. That's a very different trade than 'the Fed is done.'
items.tweetB: Two things are true at once: CPI just posted its softest read since the pandemic AND WTI brushed $80 on Hormuz risk the same day. The Fed's July decision just got easier; its September decision didn't move at all.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: subscriber section includes updated SPX/Nasdaq/Russell technicals and Fed-hike-odds tracker.

items.title: Retail hasn't started selling — it's buying 3.2x its historical pace
items.idea: Citadel's Scott Rubner data shows retail is deploying more capital than average this month, with average daily net buying running roughly 3.2x the historical monthly average. This directly contradicts the popular narrative that retail is 'finally capitulating' out of equities.
items.angle: Every pullback this year getting bought within days makes a lot more sense once you see retail running at 3.2x normal buying pace — it's not institutional dip-buying doing the work, it's retail on autopilot.
items.tweetA: Retail net buying is running ~3.2x its historical monthly average right now, per Citadel's Scott Rubner. Every fund manager survey says retail is 'finally capitulating.' The actual flow data says the opposite — they're the most aggressive structural buyer in the tape.
items.tweetB: If retail were exiting, dips would stick. They haven't. 3.2x average net buying explains why every 2% pullback this year has been bought within days — that's not institutions rotating in, that's retail on autopilot.

charts.desc: 🔥 Chart showing retail average daily net buying vs. historical monthly average — running at ~3.2x.

items.title: SPX RSI has held above 40 for 72 straight days — through a 4.5% drawdown
items.idea: The S&P 500's RSI has stayed above 40 for 72 consecutive days despite a 4.5% drawdown occurring during that same streak. Daily Chartbook's 50-year lookback shows similar setups were historically followed by near-term weakness over the following weeks, but stronger returns over longer horizons.
items.angle: A market that won't let RSI break 40 even during a real drawdown has unusually sticky underlying demand — that's typically a late-cycle behavior pattern worth tracking against how this drawdown ultimately resolves.
items.tweetA: SPX RSI has held above 40 for 72 straight days — through a 4.5% drawdown. The last 50 years of this exact setup: weaker returns over the next few weeks, stronger returns over the next year. Short-term chop, long-term fine — that's the base rate, not a prediction.
items.tweetB: A market that won't let RSI break 40 even during a real 4.5% drawdown has very sticky underlying demand. That's usually late-cycle behavior, not early-cycle — worth tracking which regime this actually resolves into.

charts.desc: 🔥 Full dashboard: 72-day RSI>40 streak overlaid with 4.5% drawdown and 50-year historical forward-return distribution.

items.title: Energy just decoupled from the rest of the market at a record level
items.idea: The trailing 1-year daily correlation between S&P 500 Energy and S&P 500 ex-Energy has turned negative for the first time since the dot-com bubble — and reached its most negative level on record, per S&P Global data.
items.angle: The last time energy decoupled like this (2000-2001), it was because tech was collapsing while energy held up. This time tech/AI is doing the carrying — worth watching which direction this decoupling breaks if the AI trade wobbles.
items.tweetA: S&P Energy vs. S&P ex-Energy correlation just went negative for the first time since the dot-com bubble — and it's now the most negative reading on record. Energy isn't trading with the market anymore, it's trading against it. That's a real diversifier again. $XLE
items.tweetB: The last time energy decoupled from the rest of the market like this was 2000-2001, when tech was collapsing while energy held up. This time tech is doing the carrying — which direction does the decoupling break when the AI trade wobbles?

charts.desc: 🔥 Chart: trailing 1-year correlation between S&P 500 Energy and S&P 500 ex-Energy, most negative on record.

items.title: Copper refuses to confirm gold's recent weakness
items.idea: Tavi Costa argues copper — which he treats as one of the most important leading indicators in the current macro environment — is not confirming gold's recent pullback. He reads that divergence as a signal the secular gold bull market remains intact rather than topping.
items.angle: Copper needs real economic growth to hold its price; gold trades more on liquidity/positioning. When a growth-sensitive metal refuses to break down alongside a safe-haven metal, that's usually the safe-haven asset's move that's temporary, not the growth metal's resilience.
items.tweetA: Copper isn't confirming gold's pullback. Tavi Costa treats that divergence as one of the cleanest leading signals in this cycle — when copper refuses to break down with gold, it's usually gold that's wrong, not copper. $GLD $CPER
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether gold's recent weakness marks the top of the bull market. The base metal that actually needs a growing economy to hold its price says no. That's a more useful signal than another gold-specific narrative.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: Tavi Costa's copper/gold ratio and leading-indicator analysis.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom™
items.title: CXMT's $9.8B IPO gives China a credible fourth DRAM player, right as Apple comes calling
items.idea: CXMT already holds roughly 8% of global DRAM revenue share with Q1 sales near $7.34B, and is IPOing at $9.8B. Apple is reportedly evaluating CXMT as an alternate DRAM supplier. CXMT's top 5 customers — Alibaba Cloud, ByteDance, Tencent, Lenovo, Xiaomi, Oppo, vivo, and Honor — generated over two-thirds of its 2025 revenue.
items.angle: China is building a captive, insulated memory supply chain in real time, right as the global HBM/DRAM shortage peaks. The moat for Samsung/SK Hynix/Micron is still EUV and HBM technology depth, but CXMT's capacity expansion plus persistent pricing discount is a real multi-year threat once the shortage eventually breaks.
items.tweetA: CXMT is IPOing at $9.8B with ~8% of global DRAM revenue and Q1 sales already at $7.34B. Apple is reportedly evaluating them as an alternate DRAM supplier. China just built a credible fourth player in a three-player memory oligopoly, right as the HBM shortage peaks. $MU
items.tweetB: CXMT's top 5 customers — Alibaba, ByteDance, Tencent, Lenovo, Xiaomi, Oppo, vivo, Honor — are over two-thirds of its revenue. That's a China-captive memory supply chain forming in real time, insulated from the same shortage squeezing Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron's Western customers.

items.title: AI regulation is converging into a toll booth incumbents can afford and startups can't
items.idea: DeepMind's Demis Hassabis is calling for a FINRA-style U.S. AI standards body to test frontier models. China's MIIT is building a parallel state safety benchmark in the same window. New York enacted the first-ever state-level one-year moratorium on new large AI data centers. Separately, AI-related debt is up 99% year over year and Amazon is returning to the bond market for $25B+.
items.angle: Every regulatory lever — model testing, state permitting, credit markets — is converging on the same choke point from a different direction, and fixed compliance costs structurally favor incumbents who can absorb them over smaller labs and open-model deployers.
items.tweetA: Hassabis wants a FINRA for frontier AI models. China is building a parallel state safety benchmark. New York just froze new AI data centers for a year. Three governments, three different levers, same target: making AI compliance a fixed cost only incumbents can absorb.
items.tweetB: AI-related debt is up 99% year over year and Amazon is going back to the bond market for $25B+. The AI story quietly moved from a venture-multiple debate to a credit-market debate, and almost nobody's pricing that shift yet.

items.title: Wells Fargo tries to price how much AI capex is already showing up in consumer bills
items.idea: A new Wells Fargo report attempts to quantify how AI-related spending is showing up in higher prices paid by everyday consumers — an attempt to isolate an actual AI-driven inflation channel rather than treat AI capex purely as an equity-market or productivity story.
items.angle: If that framework holds up, 'AI is disinflationary long-term but inflationary right now' becomes a measurable macro variable feeding into the Fed's reaction function, not just a talking point.
items.tweetA: Wells Fargo just tried to put a number on something everyone feels but nobody's quantified: how much of the price increases consumers are eating right now is AI capex passing through the supply chain. If that framework holds, it turns a talking point into a real macro variable.
items.tweetB: We keep debating whether AI capex is a bubble. Wells Fargo is asking whether it's already a hidden tax on households. Different question — and probably the more important one for the Fed's reaction function right now.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: Wells Fargo's AI-spending-to-consumer-price pass-through analysis.

items.title: The AI chip bottleneck nobody's watching: raw wafers
items.idea: Wedbush is flagging a Micron-GlobalWafers supply deal as an under-the-radar AI chip bottleneck. While the market fixates on HBM and CoWoS packaging capacity, the underlying silicon wafers feeding the entire chip supply chain may be the next chokepoint.
items.angle: The AI supply-chain story keeps moving one layer down — GPUs, then HBM, then advanced packaging, now raw wafers. Each layer down is a smaller, less-followed company with outsized leverage over the whole stack.
items.tweetA: Wedbush is flagging a Micron-GlobalWafers deal as the AI chip bottleneck nobody's watching. Everyone's tracking HBM and CoWoS capacity — the actual silicon wafers underneath all of it might be the next chokepoint. $MU $WFR
items.tweetB: The AI supply chain story keeps moving one layer down: first GPUs, then HBM, then packaging, now raw wafers. Each layer down is a smaller, less-followed company with outsized leverage over the entire stack.
